medications.indication_rationale: Once obstruction and angioedema are excluded in person, an OTC artificial-saliva substitute lubricates the dry mucosa so food no longer adheres. As interim telehealth advice it is a reasonable adjunct that gave the patient good subjective relief, but it is NOT a substitute for the urgent in-person assessment.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Topical to tongue and inner cheeks, applied approximately 5 minutes before every meal and frequently through the day as needed (per the source note; patient-directed).

medications.indication_rationale: A prescription secretagogue is a longer-term option for chronic sicca to be considered by the GP/rheumatologist — not an acute telehealth intervention for this presentation, and not to be started remotely during an unexamined acute episode.

The patient is afebrile with no exudate and no infective prodrome; prescribing antibiotics for a presumed sore throat here is an error of commission and would not address the mechanical/mucosal problem.

medications.drug_name: Antihistamine or corticosteroid for presumed allergic angioedema
medications.indication_rationale: There are no angioedema features; empirical allergy treatment is not indicated and could give false reassurance. If true angioedema/anaphylaxis develops, the correct action is an adrenaline autoinjector (if prescribed) plus 000, not an oral antihistamine as primary treatment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid dry, crumbly foods (crackers, toast) tonight and until assessed; keep food moist and take small sips of water with meals.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Dry, low-lubrication food is the trigger for mucosal adherence and the burning/gagging sensation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Chew sugar-free gum to stimulate any residual saliva production between meals.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Mechanical stimulation increases residual salivary flow and improves bolus lubrication.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do NOT try to force-swallow food, drink large amounts to 'push it down', or induce gagging to clear the sensation.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Forced clearance manoeuvres can convert a partial obstruction to complete or cause aspiration.

patient_education_points.point: Because I cannot examine your throat over chat, you need an urgent in-person check today to be sure nothing is stuck and your airway is safe — this is likely dry-mucosa friction, but that has to be confirmed by examination, not assumed.

Extreme dryness makes food scrape and stick, causing burning and a choking feeling; it does not physically close the throat over, but constant lubrication (especially before meals) is key once you have been assessed.
Sip water, keep food moist, avoid dry crumbly foods, and use artificial saliva spray frequently through the day for the underlying dry mouth.

Go to an emergency department or call 000 straight away if you become unable to swallow your own saliva, start drooling, develop a high-pitched noise when breathing in, or your lips/tongue swell or you struggle to breathe.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person assessment first; then routine GP review in 2-3 weeks once the acute episode is examined and settled
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Exclusion of food-bolus impaction/angioedema on the day; longer term, optimisation of sicca management and review of underlying autoimmune therapy
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any worsening dysphagia, inability to swallow saliva, drooling, stridor, breathing difficulty, or lip/tongue swelling — escalate immediately rather than wait

must_include_items: Recognise that acute severe dysphagia with a foreign-body/choking sensation cannot be safely excluded over telehealth
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care or ED) to examine the throat and exclude food-bolus impaction and angioedema
Screen for airway/angioedema red flags (saliva handling, drooling, stridor, lip/tongue swelling, breathing difficulty)
Give explicit airway safety-netting with a clear 000/ED trigger
acceptable_alternatives: Providing interim symptomatic advice (sip water, avoid dry foods, artificial saliva) IS acceptable only when paired with arranging urgent in-person assessment — not as stand-alone home management
Escalating directly to ED/urgent care rather than same-day GP is acceptable and safe
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange any in-person assessment and managing purely as home dry-mouth care
Failing to give airway safety-netting with an emergency trigger
Failing to screen for angioedema/anaphylaxis features
errors_of_commission: Confidently reassuring the patient that it is 'just dry mouth' with no need for examination (under-triage)
Dispatching a 000 ambulance for a patient saturating well, handling saliva, and denying airway swelling (over-triage)
Prescribing empirical antibiotics, antihistamines or corticosteroids remotely
Coaching force-swallowing or drinking to dislodge a suspected bolus
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES arranging urgent in-person assessment AND giving airway safety-netting. Reaching the benign xerostomia explanation without arranging examination is a critical under-triage failure regardless of other content.
